title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered at a repair café. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Carla, The Tool Table Organiser
Carla arrived at the repair café with a broken toaster and left as a volunteer because the welcome desk was in chaos. She liked practical systems and hated seeing people queue without knowing what to do. [47]Before each session, she had to solve the problem of a locked tool cupboard, because the caretaker often left with the only key just as volunteers were setting up.[/47] After one wasted morning, she arranged a signing-out book and a spare key box. [48]Her previous job in warehouse management helped her create an inventory that showed which screwdrivers, cables and safety glasses were available at each table.[/48] Some repairers resisted at first, saying they knew where everything was, but Carla noticed fewer arguments by the third month. She says the café changed her idea of repair: it is not only about machines working again, but about people feeling less helpless. That surprised her most.
texts.B:
### Martin, The Sewing Machine Mentor
[49]Martin was asked to join the repair café by the community centre manager after she saw him mending curtains for the theatre group upstairs.[/49] He had used sewing machines since childhood, yet teaching strangers was new to him. [50]He was surprised by the safety forms required before visitors could use needles, irons and electric machines, even under supervision.[/50] At first, he thought the rules were excessive. Then a teenager burned a sleeve slightly and he understood why the organisers wanted clear procedures. Martin’s most satisfying repair was a winter coat whose owner said she could not afford to replace it. Instead of fixing everything himself, he learned to guide people’s hands and let them make the final stitch. He says the café works best when visitors leave with a skill, not just an item, and when embarrassment turns into pride. He still remembers the first smile after a successful seam.
texts.C:
### Aisha, The Label Maker
Aisha, a product designer, volunteered after spending several Fridays waiting for her son outside the library where the repair café met. [51]During those waits, she kept noticing abandoned lamps and radios beside the bins, apparently thrown away by people who did not know help was available inside.[/51] She began by making signs for the entrance and labels for different repair stations. [55]Her labels used plain words and small drawings so visitors could find electrical, textile or bicycle help without asking awkward questions.[/55] [52]The most frustrating moment was discovering that a carefully labelled box of replacement plugs had been moved into storage and forgotten for two weeks.[/52] Aisha redesigned the system using bright tags and a wall map. She says she learned that instructions fail when they are designed for tidy minds rather than tired people carrying broken objects. Good signs, she now thinks, are a kind of kindness. every time.
texts.D:
### Peter, The Parts Salvager
[53]Peter became involved because his father, who had once repaired televisions for a living, had started losing his sight and missed the satisfaction of diagnosing faults.[/53] Peter brought him to one session, then stayed to sort donated parts. He knew little at first, but older volunteers taught him the difference between useful components and dangerous junk. [54]His hardest task was arranging noisy testing of drills, kettles and vacuum cleaners at times when the adjoining reading room was almost empty.[/54] He learned to carry machines outside when necessary and to warn librarians before anything loud began. Peter was touched when his father identified a fault by listening to a motor hum. [56]He is now preparing a guide to donated parts so the café does not depend on his growing memory of every shelf and drawer.[/56] He says repair is also a way of preserving stories. That lesson stayed with him afterwards.

questions.question: had to solve a repeated problem involving access to shared equipment?
questions.explanation: Carla had to deal with the locked tool cupboard and the missing key.

questions.question: used experience managing stock to organise equipment for others?
questions.explanation: Carla’s warehouse background helped her create an inventory of tools and safety items.

questions.question: was invited after someone noticed them doing a related practical task elsewhere in the building?
questions.explanation: Martin was asked to join after the manager saw him mending theatre curtains.

questions.question: was surprised by safety procedures before visitors could use practical equipment?
questions.explanation: Martin was surprised by the forms required before using needles, irons and electric machines.

questions.question: noticed discarded items that suggested people did not know help was available?
questions.explanation: Aisha noticed abandoned lamps and radios beside the bins while waiting outside.

questions.question: was frustrated when an organised set of materials was moved and forgotten?
questions.explanation: Aisha’s labelled box of replacement plugs was moved into storage and forgotten.

questions.question: joined because a family member missed doing a similar kind of work?
questions.explanation: Peter joined because his father missed diagnosing faults after losing his sight.

questions.question: had to arrange noisy testing so it would not disturb people nearby?
questions.explanation: Peter scheduled noisy testing when the adjoining reading room was almost empty.

questions.question: designed labels that helped visitors find the right kind of help without embarrassment?
questions.explanation: Aisha made labels with plain words and drawings for repair stations.

questions.question: is preparing guidance so future volunteers can manage donated materials without them?
questions.explanation: Peter is preparing a guide to donated parts.
